text: This article investigates the historical origins and conceptual foundations of the orthodox Trinitarian formula, primarily seeking to determine the extent to which the Nicene doctrine was shaped by Greek philosophical thought. By engaging with contemporary theological discourse, the study compares the traditional Hellenistic formulations of the Trinity against the biblical revelation of the Godhead. The research concludes that the Trinitarian formula may be better understood not as a rigid dogmatic construct, but as a profound mystery—a perspective that invites a "de-hellenization" of the concept. Ultimately, the paper argues that the Church Fathers and the post-Nicene Church adopted Greek philosophical and theological terminology largely for the purpose of contextualization, drawing upon doxological and liturgical themes already nascent within the Scriptures.
